channel.title: Quantum Crier
channel.description: Daily curated quantum computing news and insights by Mark Eatherly

item.title: Physicists turn atomic motion from a nuisance to a resource
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough represents a clever paradigm shift in trapped-ion quantum computing, where researchers have transformed atomic motion from a major source of decoherence into an actual quantum resource. Traditional approaches treat motional heating as pure noise to be suppressed, but this "erasure correction cooling" technique harnesses these atomic vibrations to store and process quantum information more reliably. The method could significantly improve error rates in ion trap systems by giving quantum computers a new way to detect and correct errors before they propagate through calculations. This work exemplifies how quantum computing advances often come from reframing fundamental challenges rather than simply trying to eliminate them.

New "erasure correction cooling" method stores quantum information in atoms’ motional states The post Physicists turn atomic motion from a nuisance to a resource appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Spin-qubit control circuit stays cool
item.description:
Curator's Take: This development addresses one of the most pressing engineering challenges in quantum computing: keeping control electronics close to qubits while maintaining the ultra-low temperatures required for quantum coherence. Traditional approaches require control signals to travel from room-temperature electronics down to millikelvin environments, introducing noise and latency that can degrade qubit performance. By developing control circuits that can operate directly in cryogenic conditions, researchers are paving the way for much more scalable quantum processors with tighter integration and potentially better fidelity. This represents a crucial step toward the kind of large-scale quantum systems needed for practical quantum advantage, where thousands or millions of qubits will require local, low-noise control.

Cryogenic architecture supports tighter integration The post Spin-qubit control circuit stays cool appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Helgoland 2025: the inside story of what happened on the ‘quantum island’
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article captures a historic moment as physicists gathered on the remote German island of Helgoland to commemorate the centennial of Werner Heisenberg's groundbreaking work that launched quantum mechanics in 1925. The conference brings together leading quantum researchers to reflect on how far the field has traveled from Heisenberg's original matrix mechanics to today's quantum computers, sensors, and communication systems that are reshaping technology. Beyond the symbolic importance of returning to quantum mechanics' birthplace, such gatherings offer valuable insights into where prominent physicists see the field heading next, making this both a celebration of past achievements and a roadmap for quantum's second century. The timing is particularly poignant as we stand at the threshold of practical quantum advantage in multiple applications, from cryptography to drug discovery.

Matin Durrani reports from the Helgoland 2025 conference marking 100 years of quantum mechanics The post Helgoland 2025: the inside story of what happened on the ‘quantum island’ appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Gino Elia: ‘I worry that scientists are too confident that the acceptance of scientific truth is inevitable’
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article raises important questions about scientific communication and public trust that are particularly relevant to quantum computing, where complex concepts like quantum supremacy and error correction often face skepticism or misunderstanding. As quantum technologies move closer to practical applications in cryptography, drug discovery, and financial modeling, the field cannot afford to assume that revolutionary capabilities will be automatically accepted by society or policymakers. Elia's concerns echo broader challenges quantum researchers face when explaining counterintuitive phenomena like superposition and entanglement to stakeholders who will ultimately decide funding priorities and regulatory frameworks. The quantum computing community would benefit from heeding these warnings about scientific overconfidence, especially as we approach the critical transition from laboratory demonstrations to real-world quantum advantage.

Following the Helgoland 2025 meeting in June, Gino Elia leaves with concerns about the culture of science The post Gino Elia: ‘I worry that scientists are too confident that the acceptance of scientific truth is inevitable’ appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum island: why Helgoland is a great spot for fundamental thinking
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights the ongoing Helgoland 2025 conference, where physicists are gathering on the historic North Sea island where Werner Heisenberg first formulated matrix mechanics in 1925 to tackle fundamental questions about quantum mechanics that remain unresolved a century later. The location is deeply symbolic - Helgoland was where the mathematical foundations of quantum theory were born during Heisenberg's famous hay fever retreat, making it an inspired venue for contemplating the deepest mysteries that still puzzle quantum theorists today. While quantum computing has made remarkable practical strides, conferences like this remind us that foundational questions about measurement, interpretation, and the nature of quantum reality continue to drive scientific inquiry and could influence how we understand and develop quantum technologies. The gathering demonstrates how the quantum community remains committed to wrestling with the profound philosophical and physical questions that underpin all quantum applications, from computing to sensing.

Matin Durrani explains why delegates at Helgoland 2025 are discussing foundational questions in quantum mechanics The post Quantum island: why Helgoland is a great spot for fundamental thinking appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Superconducting innovation: SQMS shapes up for scalable success in quantum computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights Fermilab's SQMS Center's ambitious push toward qudit-based quantum computing, which could represent a significant leap beyond today's qubit systems by utilizing quantum states with more than two levels per unit. The focus on qudits is particularly intriguing because these higher-dimensional quantum states could dramatically increase computational density and potentially simplify certain quantum algorithms, though they come with added complexity in control and readout. What makes this especially noteworthy is SQMS's emphasis on inherent scalability from the ground up, addressing one of quantum computing's most persistent challenges by designing systems that can grow without hitting fundamental roadblocks. This research direction could prove crucial as the field moves toward fault-tolerant quantum computers that require thousands or millions of quantum units working together.

Fermilab’s SQMS Center is on a mission to realize inherently scalable “qudit-based” quantum computing and quantum communication systems The post Superconducting innovation: SQMS shapes up for scalable success in quantum computing appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Teaching quantum physics to everyone: pictures offer a new way of understanding
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article addresses one of quantum computing's biggest challenges: making quantum mechanics accessible to broader audiences beyond physics specialists. As quantum technologies move closer to practical applications, the ability to communicate quantum concepts clearly becomes crucial for educating the workforce, informing policy decisions, and building public understanding of this transformative field. Visual approaches to teaching quantum mechanics could prove especially valuable as quantum computing moves from research labs into industry, where engineers, software developers, and business leaders need to grasp quantum principles without necessarily having advanced physics backgrounds. Better educational tools for quantum concepts will be essential for scaling the quantum ecosystem and ensuring that the quantum advantage can be realized across diverse sectors.

We talk about a new way of describing quantum mechanics The post Teaching quantum physics to everyone: pictures offer a new way of understanding appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: The quantum eraser doesn’t rewrite the past – it rewrites observers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This Physics World article tackles one of quantum mechanics' most mind-bending phenomena - the quantum eraser experiment - and offers a fresh perspective on what it really tells us about the nature of measurement and observation. Rather than suggesting we can retroactively change the past (a common misinterpretation), Violaris argues that quantum erasers reveal how the act of measurement fundamentally shapes what we can know about a quantum system's history. This distinction is crucial for quantum computing applications, where understanding the precise role of measurement in quantum information processing directly impacts how we design quantum algorithms and error correction schemes. The article provides valuable clarity on a concept that often gets sensationalized, helping readers grasp the true quantum mechanical principles that underpin technologies from quantum sensors to quantum computers.

Maria Violaris looks to the past and future of quantum observers and measurements The post The quantum eraser doesn’t rewrite the past – it rewrites observers appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: ‘We must prioritize continuity and stability to maintain momentum’: Mauro Paternostro on how to ensure that quantum tech continues to thrive
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article captures a critical moment in quantum computing's evolution, as the field transitions from pure research breakthroughs to sustained technological development requiring long-term investment strategies. Paternostro's emphasis on continuity and stability reflects growing industry awareness that quantum advantage won't emerge from sporadic funding bursts, but rather from consistent, patient capital that allows complex quantum systems to mature over multiple development cycles. His perspective is particularly timely as governments and corporations worldwide are making billion-dollar quantum commitments, yet many programs still operate on short funding cycles that can disrupt the deep, methodical work quantum technologies demand. The insights offer valuable guidance for policymakers and industry leaders navigating the delicate balance between maintaining innovation momentum and building the stable ecosystem quantum computing needs to fulfill its transformative potential.

Quantum physicist Mauro Paternostro shares his views on the quantum future The post ‘We must prioritize continuity and stability to maintain momentum’: Mauro Paternostro on how to ensure that quantum tech continues to thrive appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: India must boost investment in quantum technologies to become world leader, says report
item.description:
Curator's Take: This report from India's National Quantum Mission highlights the increasingly competitive global race for quantum supremacy, with nations recognizing that early investment and strategic planning will determine future technological leadership. India's emphasis on both advancing quantum technologies and strengthening quantum security reflects a mature understanding that quantum computing will simultaneously create new capabilities and vulnerabilities that countries must prepare for simultaneously. The timing is particularly significant as India seeks to position itself alongside the US, China, and EU in quantum research while addressing the critical need for quantum-safe cryptography before large-scale quantum computers threaten current security systems. This dual focus on offensive quantum capabilities and defensive quantum security measures represents exactly the kind of comprehensive national strategy that experts believe will separate quantum leaders from followers in the coming decade.

First report by the National Quantum Mission also warns that India needs to improve its quantum security The post India must boost investment in quantum technologies to become world leader, says report appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum transducer enables optical control of a superconducting qubit
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough represents a major step toward practical quantum networking by solving one of the field's most persistent challenges: connecting superconducting qubits, which operate at microwave frequencies, with optical photons used for long-distance quantum communication. The quantum transducer essentially acts as a translator between these two incompatible "languages," enabling researchers to control superconducting qubits directly with light for the first time. This development could dramatically simplify the architecture of distributed quantum computers and quantum networks, eliminating the need for complex electronic intermediaries that introduce noise and reduce fidelity. The work brings us closer to a future where superconducting quantum processors can be seamlessly networked using existing fiber optic infrastructure.

Physicists create the first device that can control a superconducting microwave qubit using only light The post Quantum transducer enables optical control of a superconducting qubit appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: On the path towards a quantum economy
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a crucial phase in quantum computing's evolution where the field is transitioning from pure research to practical business applications through systematic feasibility studies. These collaborative efforts between quantum specialists and industry experts represent a vital bridge-building exercise, helping companies understand which quantum computing advantages might genuinely benefit their operations versus areas where classical computing remains superior. The emphasis on feasibility studies is particularly significant because it suggests the quantum computing industry is maturing beyond the hype cycle into rigorous, evidence-based assessments of where quantum advantage can realistically emerge. This collaborative approach could accelerate the timeline for meaningful quantum applications by ensuring development efforts focus on problems where quantum computers can deliver measurable business value rather than pursuing quantum solutions simply for their novelty.

Feasibility studies are enabling industry experts to collaborate with quantum specialists to discover the potential benefits of quantum computing for their businesses and their customers The post On the path towards a quantum economy appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Schrödinger cat states like it hot
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough demonstrates that quantum superposition states can survive at surprisingly high temperatures of 1.8 Kelvin, which is remarkably warm by quantum standards where most experiments require near absolute zero conditions. The ability to maintain these delicate "Schrödinger cat" states at higher temperatures could dramatically reduce the cooling requirements for quantum computers, potentially making them more practical and less expensive to operate. This finding challenges conventional wisdom about the fragility of quantum states and opens new possibilities for quantum devices that don't require extreme refrigeration, bringing us closer to room-temperature quantum computing applications. The research represents a significant step toward making quantum technology more accessible by relaxing one of its most demanding technical requirements.

Superpositions persist at temperatures up to 1.8 K – practically oven-like by quantum standards The post Schrödinger cat states like it hot appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: The quantum Zeno effect: how the ‘measurement problem’ went from philosophers’ paradox to physicists’ toolbox
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article explores how the quantum Zeno effect, named after the ancient Greek philosopher's paradox about motion, has evolved from a theoretical curiosity into a practical quantum technology tool. The effect demonstrates that frequent quantum measurements can actually freeze a quantum system's evolution, which initially seemed like just another bizarre quantum phenomenon but now serves as the foundation for enhanced quantum sensing applications and error correction schemes. What makes this particularly fascinating is how it bridges the gap between quantum mechanics' most counterintuitive aspects and real-world utility, showing that even the "measurement problem" that has puzzled physicists for decades can become an engineering advantage. The transformation from philosophical puzzle to technological asset perfectly illustrates how our understanding of quantum mechanics continues to mature into actionable quantum technologies.

A stand-out example of quantum weirdness is turning out to be surprisingly useful, as Margaret Harris explains The post The quantum Zeno effect: how the ‘measurement problem’ went from philosophers’ paradox to physicists’ toolbox appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum computer generates strings of certifiably random numbers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough demonstrates one of quantum computing's most practically valuable near-term applications: generating truly random numbers that can be mathematically certified as unpredictable, unlike the pseudo-random numbers produced by classical computers. Quantinuum's achievement using trapped-ion technology showcases how quantum systems can leverage fundamental quantum mechanical properties like superposition and measurement uncertainty to create genuine randomness, which is crucial for cryptography, secure communications, and scientific simulations. The fact that this work also extends to studying quantum magnetism and knot theory highlights how today's quantum computers are becoming versatile research tools capable of tackling multiple complex problems simultaneously. This represents a significant step toward practical quantum advantage in applications where certified randomness provides clear benefits over classical alternatives.

Quantinuum’s trapped-ion computer has also been used to study problems in quantum magnetism and knot theory The post Quantum computer generates strings of certifiably random numbers appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: New entanglement approach could boost photonic quantum computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This theoretical breakthrough could address one of photonic quantum computing's biggest headaches: the probabilistic nature of photon entanglement that has plagued efforts to scale up light-based quantum systems. By leveraging non-Abelian holonomy - essentially exploiting how quantum states change when moved around closed paths in parameter space - researchers propose a way to create entangled photon pairs deterministically rather than relying on the hit-or-miss approaches that currently dominate the field. This matters enormously because deterministic entanglement could dramatically improve the success rates of photonic quantum gates, potentially making room-temperature photonic quantum computers more viable competitors to their superconducting cousins. While still theoretical, this approach represents the kind of mathematical elegance that often translates into practical quantum computing advantages down the road.

Harnessing the mathematical concept of non-Abelian holonomy makes it possible to entangle photons deterministically, say theorists The post New entanglement approach could boost photonic quantum computing appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: William Phillips: Nobel laureate talks about his passion for quantum physics
item.description:
Curator's Take: This interview with Nobel Prize winner William Phillips offers valuable perspective from one of the foundational figures in quantum technology, whose pioneering work in laser cooling of atoms laid crucial groundwork for today's quantum computing and sensing applications. Phillips' insights are particularly relevant as the field grapples with scaling challenges, since his techniques for precisely controlling atomic motion remain central to trapped-ion quantum computers and atomic clocks that serve as quantum sensors. The article provides readers with a rare glimpse into how a quantum physics pioneer views the current state and future potential of the technologies his research helped make possible. Given Phillips' decades of experience bridging fundamental physics and practical applications, his thoughts on where quantum technologies are headed carry significant weight in understanding the field's trajectory.

Laser-cooling expert looks to the future of quantum technologies The post William Phillips: Nobel laureate talks about his passion for quantum physics appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Operating system for quantum networks is a first
item.description:
Curator's Take: This development represents a crucial step toward making quantum networks as user-friendly as classical computer networks, addressing one of the biggest barriers to widespread quantum adoption. QNodeOS could standardize how different quantum devices communicate and operate together, much like how traditional operating systems allow various software applications to run on different hardware platforms. The creation of a dedicated quantum network operating system suggests the field is maturing from experimental demonstrations toward practical, scalable infrastructure that non-experts could eventually use. This kind of abstraction layer is essential for quantum computing to transition from specialized research labs to broader commercial and academic applications.

QNodeOS could help make quantum computation more accessible The post Operating system for quantum networks is a first appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Microsoft’s Chetan Nayak on topological qubits, the physics of bigger splashes
item.description:
Curator's Take: This Physics World podcast with Microsoft's Chetan Nayak offers valuable insights into one of quantum computing's most ambitious hardware approaches - topological qubits based on Majorana zero modes. Microsoft has been pursuing this challenging but potentially revolutionary path for over a decade, betting that topological protection could provide inherently error-resistant qubits that would dramatically reduce the overhead needed for quantum error correction. While Microsoft's topological quantum computing program has faced significant technical hurdles and timeline delays, recent progress in materials science and fabrication techniques has renewed optimism about this approach. The discussion likely provides important updates on Microsoft's current progress and the fundamental physics challenges that make topological qubits both incredibly difficult to realize and potentially game-changing for fault-tolerant quantum computing.

Manu jumping and Majorana zero modes feature in this podcast The post Microsoft’s Chetan Nayak on topological qubits, the physics of bigger splashes appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum computers extend lead over classical machines in random circuit sampling
item.description:
Curator's Take: This latest achievement with the Zuchongzhi 3.0 processor represents another significant milestone in demonstrating quantum supremacy, showing that quantum computers continue to widen their advantage over classical systems in specific computational tasks. Random circuit sampling, while not immediately practical, serves as a crucial benchmark for proving quantum computers can outperform even the world's most powerful supercomputers by astronomical margins - in this case, reducing billions of years of classical computation to mere minutes. The result reinforces that we're witnessing genuine quantum advantage in action and validates the rapid progress being made in quantum hardware development, particularly in photonic and superconducting quantum systems. While these demonstrations don't yet translate to solving real-world problems faster than classical computers, they provide essential proof that quantum computing is moving from theoretical possibility to computational reality.

New Zuchongzhi 3.0 processor can solve in minutes a problem that would take a supercomputer billions of years The post Quantum computers extend lead over classical machines in random circuit sampling appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly
